note: Astro research. Only one existing Astro post on the blog (astro-islands-vs-react-server-components-guide, a conceptual comparison), so these topics go wide into Astro-specific build/decision territory. Big 2026 context: Cloudflare acquired Astro (Jan 2026); Astro 6 stable (Feb 2026) with Content Layer, Server Islands, and Live Content Collections.

topics.title: Cloudflare Bought Astro: What It Actually Means for Your Stack
topics.whyTrending: January 2026 acquisition — the entire Astro team joined Cloudflare, framework stays MIT. Strategy/opinion content performs well on the blog (see the Anthropic-Bun piece) and this is the single hottest Astro story of the year.

topics.title: Astro Server Islands in Practice: Personalization on a Static Shell
topics.whyTrending: Server Islands (server:defer) are Astro's answer to the static-vs-dynamic tension — cache the shell at the edge, defer only the personalized bits (cart, avatar, reviews). Highly demo-able and central to Astro 5/6.

topics.title: Astro Content Layer: Type-Safe Content from Markdown to CMS to DB
topics.whyTrending: The Content Layer API is Astro 5's headline feature — one typed, pluggable API over markdown, MDX, headless CMS, and databases. Core to why content teams pick Astro; strong SEO for 'astro content collections'.

topics.title: Migrating a Next.js Blog to Astro: When It's Worth It (and When It's Not)
topics.whyTrending: Content sites are leaving Next.js for Astro (90% less JS, 2-3x faster loads); migration is typically 1-3 days with a compatible MDX pipeline. Pairs directly with the blog's heavy Next.js catalog and has strong search intent.

topics.title: Astro vs Next.js 16 in 2026: The Honest Content-vs-App Decision
topics.whyTrending: The perennial comparison, now sharpened post-Cloudflare-acquisition and Astro 6. The blog owns tons of Next.js authority, so an even-handed 'which and when' is a natural, high-traffic piece.

topics.title: Live Content Collections: Request-Time Data in Astro 6
topics.whyTrending: Astro 6 split content collections into build-time and live (request-time) via defineLiveCollection() in src/live.config.ts — a genuinely new capability that blurs the content/app line. Fresh, low competition.

topics.title: Astro View Transitions: Silky Page Animations Without a SPA
topics.whyTrending: Astro's view-transitions give SPA-like navigation on an MPA with transition:name morphing and lifecycle events — the pitfalls (persistence, scripts re-running) are the real content. Complements the blog's existing View Transitions API piece.

topics.title: E-commerce with Astro: Static Catalog, Server-Island Cart
topics.whyTrending: Astro's static-shell + server-islands model is a natural fit for storefronts — cache the product catalog globally, defer cart/pricing/reviews per user. Practical, demo-able, and commercially relevant.

topics.title: One App, Many Frameworks: Astro Islands with React, Vue, and Svelte
topics.whyTrending: Astro's ability to run React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid components side by side (with client: directives) is a genuinely unique selling point and a migration lifeline — underexplained in practical, gotcha-aware depth.

topics.title: Deploying Astro to Cloudflare Workers: Edge, D1, and R2 After the Acquisition
topics.whyTrending: Post-acquisition, Astro on Cloudflare Workers gets first-class edge rendering plus D1 (database) and R2 (storage) integration. Timely, practical, and ties the #1 acquisition story to a concrete build.
